steps.title: Identify the three-item series
steps.content:
The sentence lists three pieces of data being shown: length (62 km), slip rate (between 0.2 and 1.0 mm/yr), and most recent deformation (less than 15 thousand years ago).

In Standard English, a three-item series requires proper punctuation to separate items and parenthetical details must attach directly to their nouns.

steps.title: Apply the correct series punctuation
steps.content:
The correct answer correctly keeps parenthetical details attached directly to their nouns—length (62 km), slip rate (between 0.2 and 1.0 mm/yr)—then uses commas to separate all three series items before and most recent deformation.

The wording “length, (62 km); slip rate, (between 0.2 and 1.0 mm/yr);” inserts extraneous commas between each noun and its parenthetical detail and uses semicolons in place of commas to separate series items. “length, (62 km), slip rate, (between 0.2 and 1.0 mm/yr),” inserts extraneous commas between each noun and its parenthetical. “length (62 km) slip rate (between 0.2 and 1.0 mm/yr)” omits all punctuation between series items entirely.
